reasoning: Locative of hiseeθ: θ becomes t (as in obviative), then adds -owe? locative suffix pattern
Singular of ooto: removes plural -o suffix
Plural of coox: adds -o? suffix (or possibly -ino?)
Obviative of coox: adds -i suffix
Locative of coox: adds -owe? suffix
Obviative of hinen: changes -en to -eno
Locative of hinen: adds -inowe? suffix based on plural ino pattern
Plural of wotoo: adds -no? suffix
Obviative of wotoo: adds -w suffix
Singular of woθonohoeno: removes plural -no suffix
Obviative of woθonohoe: adds -o suffix
Singular form derived from obviative niiʔeihiio by removing -o
Plural of niiʔeihii: adds -ino? suffix
Locative of niiʔeihii: adds -ine? suffix based on -ino plural
Obviative of ceʔibes: ceʔ→hii, bes→beso pattern
Locative of ceʔibes: based on plural -xo, adds -xe? locative
Plural of benes: adds -ino? suffix
Obviative of benes: be→hie, nes→neso pattern
Locative of benes: based on plural -ino?, adds -ine? locative
Singular of nesiho?: removes plural -ho? suffix
Obviative of nesi: ne→hi, si→bio pattern (same as nebi→hibio)
Locative of nesi: based on plural -ho?, adds -howeʔ locative

answer: The patterns show: (1) Plurals add suffixes like -no?, -wo?, -ho?, -ino, -to, etc. depending on the stem; (2) Obviatives often involve vowel changes or additions (e.g., i→in, adding -o, -w); (3) Locatives build on plural forms by adding -we? or -e? suffixes. The obviative sometimes shows consonant/vowel mutations (ne→hi pattern seen in nebi→hibio).
